title: Mobile RV Repair Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Mobile RV Repair Business — Costs, Earnings, Steps
metaDescription: Honest guide to starting a mobile RV repair business: real startup costs, RVIA/RVTI certification, realistic earnings, seasonality, and what most people get wrong.
bestFor: Hands-on, systems-minded people who can troubleshoot multiple trades and like working on the road
biggestRisk: Taking jobs beyond your skill, botching a repair, and damaging an expensive coach or your reputation
whatItIs: A mobile RV repair business fixes and maintains recreational vehicles at the customer's location — their driveway, a campground, an RV park, or the roadside — instead of in a shop. RVs are essentially small houses on wheels, so the work spans appliances (fridges, water heaters, furnaces, AC), the 12V and 120V electrical systems, plumbing and tanks, slide-out mechanisms, roof and seal maintenance, leveling systems, and propane. This is distinct from an auto mechanic: you rarely touch the engine or drivetrain on a motorhome, and most clients have towable trailers with no engine at all. Owners pay a premium for mobile service because hauling an RV to a dealership often means weeks-long waits, so coming to them is the whole value proposition.
dailyReality: You drive a stocked service van between two and five appointments a day, diagnosing and fixing whatever the RV throws at you — one stop might be a non-cooling absorption fridge, the next a slide-out that won't retract, the next resealing a leaking roof. You diagnose, source or carry the right parts, and repair on site, often outdoors and in tight spaces or on a roof. Around the wrenching, expect real time on phone and text triage (deciding what's worth a visit), ordering parts, writing estimates and invoices, and scheduling. Because RV owners travel, a chunk of your work is seasonal and clustered around camping season and big departures and returns, and some jobs require travel to campgrounds an hour or more away.

startupCostsItemized.item: Service van — used, reliable (or use one you own)
Van shelving, organization, and power setup
Tools — multimeters, refrigeration, plumbing, sealing, general
Initial parts and consumables inventory (seals, fittings, common spares)
General liability and commercial auto insurance
Business registration / LLC and any local trade requirements
Diagnostic software, manuals, Google Business Profile, scheduling

realEarnings.yearOne: Most operators earn $3,000 to $7,000 per month in year one, ramping as word spreads and skills broaden. Labor rates commonly run $100 to $175 per hour plus a service-call/trip fee, but early on you lose billable time to learning, parts runs, and slow scheduling.
realEarnings.experienced: Experienced techs with strong reviews and a broad skill set commonly earn $7,000 to $14,000 per month solo in season, billing $125 to $200+ per hour plus trip fees and parts markup. Warranty and insurance work, when you can get authorized, adds volume.
realEarnings.topEarners: Top operators run $18,000 to $40,000+ per month by adding trucks and techs, securing manufacturer warranty authorization, and serving dense RV markets and large parks under contract. Reaching this means hiring scarce qualified techs, carrying more inventory, and managing logistics — and seasonality still swings revenue hard.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Skill breadth and diagnostic speed matter most — the tech who can confidently fix appliances, electrical, and slide-outs bills far more than one who only does basics. Trip-fee discipline, parts markup, route density, and climate (year-round vs. seasonal) heavily shape income.
realEarnings.perHour: Posted labor rates are $100 to $200 per hour, but counting driving, diagnosis, parts runs, quoting, and slow winter weeks, realistic blended earnings often work out to $50 to $100 per hour of total time, higher for efficient, well-routed techs.

howToStart: Month 1: Get trained and credentialed. Pursue RVIA/RVTI training and certification (RV Technician levels) — it is not strictly required everywhere but dramatically shortens the learning curve and builds customer and warranty trust. Without real hands-on systems aptitude, build skills first; this is not a from-scratch beginner business.

Month 2: Set up the rig and the back office. Outfit a reliable van with shelving, tools, and a starter parts inventory, secure general liability and commercial auto insurance, register the business, and set your labor rate, trip fee, and parts policy.

Days 60–120: Get your first jobs. Build a Google Business Profile, list on mobile-RV-tech directories and RV repair apps, and introduce yourself to nearby RV parks, campgrounds, and dealerships with backlogs that may refer overflow. Take clear before/after photos and ask every happy customer for a review.

Days 120–180: Broaden your skills toward the high-value work (refrigeration, slide-outs, electrical), track your true time and parts costs per job, and pursue warranty/insurance authorization. Decide whether seasonality in your area means adding off-season services or work.

mustHave: Genuine multi-trade aptitude — comfortable with electrical, plumbing, appliances, and mechanical systems
Strong diagnostic skill: finding the actual fault efficiently rather than swapping parts and guessing
Customer-facing professionalism and clear communication while working at people's homes and campsites
canLearn: RV-specific systems and quirks (absorption fridges, slide mechanisms, leveling, seals) via RVTI courses and the manuals
Proper estimating, trip-fee, and parts-markup practices to stay profitable
Warranty and insurance claim processes and the documentation they require
separators: Skill breadth and confidence on high-value repairs (refrigeration, slide-outs, electrical) that lower-skilled techs avoid
Manufacturer warranty authorization, which provides steady, higher-volume work
Reputation for honest diagnosis and clean work, which drives referrals in tight-knit RV communities

whatPeopleGetWrong: Taking on systems they don't actually understand, then botching a repair on an expensive coach and torching their reputation
Treating it like auto mechanic work — RVs are houses on wheels, and the systems and codes are different
Forgetting to charge a proper trip/service-call fee, so windshield time and parts runs quietly destroy the margin
Underestimating seasonality and cash-flow swings, then getting caught short during the slow off-season
Carrying too little inventory, so jobs stall waiting on parts and the customer's RV (and their trip) stays broken
Skipping RVTI training and insurance, which limits the work they can win and exposes them on costly mistakes

toolsAndEquipment.note: Your shop on wheels. Shelving and a parts system save hours; start with a van you own if you can.
Quality multimeter, clamp meter, and test gear. Diagnosis speed is where you make or lose money.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Trade tools and specialty RV tools
toolsAndEquipment.note: Plumbing, sealing, refrigeration, and slide-out tools beyond a basic mechanic's kit.
Common seals, fittings, fuses, and spares so you can fix it on the first visit.
Manufacturer manuals and subscriptions; RV systems are documented and varied.
Jobber, Housecall Pro, or similar to manage appointments, estimates, and card payments in the field.

methods: A complete Google Business Profile with reviews and before/after photos — the top driver of local RV-owner leads
Listings on mobile-RV-tech directories and RV repair apps where owners actively search for help
Relationships with RV parks, campgrounds, and storage lots that field constant repair requests
Referrals from dealerships with long service backlogs that hand off out-of-warranty or overflow work
RV owner Facebook groups and forums where members ask for trusted mobile techs by region
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Customers are RV owners — full-timers, weekend campers, and travelers passing through — concentrated near campgrounds, RV parks, storage lots, and popular routes, especially in camping season and in warm-climate winter destinations. Parks and dealerships are key referral channels.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: First jobs typically come within one to three months of marketing and a couple of park introductions. A steady, referral-fed client base usually takes a full season to establish, since demand clusters around camping season and travel patterns.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Generic print ads and broad social campaigns before you have reviews and directory listings. RV owners search online and rely on community recommendations, so a strong profile and park relationships beat untargeted advertising.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes, readily, in markets with enough RVs and a longer season. A skilled solo tech can fill a full-time schedule in season; the constraint is daylight, your skill breadth, and the off-season slowdown in cold climates.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Possible but hard, because qualified RV techs are genuinely scarce. Adding trucks and techs multiplies capacity and lets you pursue warranty contracts, but recruiting, training, and trusting techs with expensive coaches is the main bottleneck to stepping back.
howItScales.sellable: Yes, more than many solo trades. A business with multiple trucks, warranty authorizations, park and dealer relationships, recurring customers, and documented processes sells for a real multiple. A one-person operation tied to the owner's skill is harder to transfer.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Recruiting and training scarce qualified techs, more stocked vans, manufacturer warranty authorization, inventory and logistics systems, and a marketing engine that fills schedules across multiple trucks despite seasonality.

strongFit: You have real multi-trade, hands-on aptitude and enjoy diagnosing varied problems
You like mobile, independent work and don't mind driving and working outdoors or on roofs
You are willing to invest in RVTI training and keep learning systems
You can manage seasonal income swings and run a tidy back office
poorFit: You have no mechanical/systems background and want a true beginner business
You want predictable, identical work rather than varied troubleshooting
You can't handle seasonal cash-flow swings or off-season slowdowns
You're uncomfortable taking responsibility for repairs on $50,000-plus vehicles
askYourself: Am I genuinely confident across electrical, plumbing, appliances, and mechanical systems, or only one of them?
Will I charge a proper trip fee and track parts and time so windshield time doesn't eat my margin?
Does my area have enough RVs and a long enough season to keep me busy, or will winter be lean?

faqs.question: Do I need to be a certified RV technician to start?
faqs.answer: Certification is not legally required in most places, but RVIA/RVTI training and the RV Technician certifications dramatically shorten the learning curve, build customer trust, and are often needed for manufacturer warranty authorization. Given RV systems are specialized and the vehicles are expensive, starting with no relevant background and no training is a recipe for costly mistakes.

faqs.question: How is this different from being an auto mechanic?
faqs.answer: An RV is a small house on wheels, so most work is appliances, electrical, plumbing, slide-outs, seals, and propane rather than engines and drivetrains. Many clients have towable trailers with no engine at all. The skill set overlaps with handyman and trades work more than with automotive repair, which is why dedicated RV training matters.

faqs.question: How much should I charge?
faqs.answer: Mobile RV techs commonly charge $100 to $200 per hour for labor, plus a service-call or trip fee to cover travel, plus parts with a markup. The trip fee is essential because you spend real time driving between sites and chasing parts. Underpricing or skipping the trip fee is one of the fastest ways to go broke while looking busy.

faqs.question: Is the work seasonal?
faqs.answer: In most of the country, yes — demand peaks in camping season (spring through fall) and around major travel periods, then slows in winter. Warm-climate areas with snowbird RV traffic can run closer to year-round. Plan your cash flow for the off-season, and consider complementary work or winterizing services to fill the gap.

faqs.question: Why do RV owners pay extra for mobile service?
faqs.answer: Because the alternative is towing the RV to a dealership and often waiting weeks for a service slot, sometimes missing trips entirely. Coming to the owner's driveway, campsite, or storage lot and fixing it on the spot is the core value, and owners will pay a premium for the convenience and speed.

faqs.question: What is the biggest mistake new RV techs make?
faqs.answer: Taking on systems they don't fully understand and botching a repair on an expensive coach, which damages both the customer's rig and their own reputation in a tight-knit community. The honest approach is to know your limits, keep expanding your training, and refer out what you can't yet do well.

faqs.question: Can I run this part-time around a job?
faqs.answer: It is difficult. RV owners often need repairs on tight timelines tied to upcoming trips, and quality diagnosis, parts sourcing, and travel take real time during the day. You can ease in part-time to build skills and reviews, but the business realistically wants near full-time attention to serve customers well and stay profitable.

dataSources: RV Industry Association (RVIA) and RV Training Institute (RVTI) certification and training program documentation
U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics — installation, maintenance, and repair occupational data
RV repair labor-rate and cost guides from industry and owner resources
Operator communities and RV owner forums (iRV2, mobile-RV-tech groups) for real-world rates and seasonality
